past_medical_history: Migraine without aura, diagnosed at age 22, infrequent episodes (2-3 per year)
Patent foramen ovale found incidentally on echocardiogram during college sports physical, not repaired
Mild intermittent asthma, childhood onset, well-controlled
Cervical chiropractic manipulation 3 days ago for neck stiffness after a long drive
family_history: Paternal grandmother with stroke at age 79
No family history of early cardiovascular disease or clotting disorders

social_history.exercise: Plays recreational soccer once weekly, jogs 2-3 times per week
social_history.illicit_drugs: Occasional marijuana use, denies other substances
social_history.travel: Drove 8 hours for a conference 4 days ago

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Mildly anxious. Speech is fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Naming, repetition, and comprehension intact. Attention and registration normal. Able to recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally, equal and reactive. Visual fields full to confrontation. Subtle right-beating horizontal nystagmus on left lateral gaze, direction-changing component noted on right lateral gaze. No skew deviation on alternate cover test initially noted, but subtle left hypertropia elicited on repeat testing. Facial sensation and motor function symmetric. Hearing grossly normal bilaterally. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 throughout bilateral upper and lower extremities. No pronator drift. Normal bulk and tone.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Decreased vibration sense in the left foot compared to the right. Proprioception intact in all extremities.
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout bilateral upper and lower extremities. Plantar responses are flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Left-sided dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing, more pronounced with eyes closed. Left heel-to-shin test with subtle overshoot. Right-sided coordination intact. Rapid alternating movements mildly impaired on the left compared to the right.
neurological_exam.gait: Wide-based gait with tendency to veer to the left. Unable to tandem walk, falling to the left after 2 steps. Romberg test positive with leftward sway.
neurological_exam.additional: NIHSS score: 3. HINTS exam: horizontal nystagmus is direction-changing (central pattern), positive head impulse test bilaterally (could suggest peripheral etiology but inconsistent with direction-changing nystagmus), subtle skew deviation present on repeated testing. Negative Dix-Hallpike bilaterally.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden onset of severe dizziness, double vision, and difficulty walking
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Rafael Dominguez is a 35-year-old right-handed man with a history of infrequent migraines and a known patent foramen ovale who presents to the emergency department via private vehicle after the acute onset of severe vertigo, diplopia, and gait unsteadiness beginning approximately 90 minutes ago. The patient states he was sitting at his desk at work when he experienced sudden onset of the room spinning violently, accompanied by nausea and one episode of vomiting. He initially attributed this to a possible inner ear problem, as he had experienced a brief episode of positional dizziness about a year ago that resolved spontaneously. However, within minutes he noticed horizontal double vision that worsened when looking to the left, and when he tried to stand, he felt as though he was being pulled to the left and could not walk in a straight line. A coworker drove him to the emergency department. He denies headache at the onset, although he now reports mild occipital discomfort. He denies any hearing loss, tinnitus, facial weakness, limb weakness, numbness, or speech difficulty. He notes mild neck soreness that has been present since receiving chiropractic cervical manipulation three days ago for stiffness that developed after an eight-hour drive. He has not had any recent febrile illness, upper respiratory infection, or ear infection. He took ibuprofen 400 mg about one hour ago without relief. He has not used sumatriptan because the symptoms did not feel like his typical migraines, which usually involve unilateral pulsating headache with photophobia. The triage nurse initially categorized him as a likely vestibular neuritis case. On further questioning, the patient mentions that the dizziness is constant and not positional, and that the double vision persists regardless of head position.

findings.location: Left lateral medulla and left inferior cerebellar peduncle

signal_characteristics.ADC: Corresponding hypointensity confirming true diffusion restriction
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Equivocal subtle hyperintensity, may be too early for definitive FLAIR change
signal_characteristics.T2: Subtle hyperintensity in left lateral medulla

findings.borders: Poorly defined margins in a distribution consistent with posterior inferior cerebellar artery territory

signal_characteristics.MRA: Irregular narrowing of the left vertebral artery at the V3 segment with a tapered appearance suggestive of dissection
signal_characteristics.T1_fat_sat: Crescent-shaped hyperintensity surrounding the left vertebral artery lumen at V3 level, consistent with intramural hematoma

additional_observations: No additional areas of restricted diffusion
Right vertebral artery and basilar artery are patent with normal caliber on MRA
No evidence of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome
Normal appearance of the cerebral hemispheres, deep gray nuclei, and supratentorial white matter
mri.impression: 1. Acute left lateral medullary and inferior cerebellar peduncle infarction in the left PICA territory with restricted diffusion. 2. Left vertebral artery V3 segment irregularity with intramural hematoma on fat-saturated T1, highly suggestive of vertebral artery dissection as the embolic source. 3. No hemorrhagic transformation. This constellation of findings is consistent with a vertebral artery dissection causing posterior circulation ischemic stroke.

differential_by_imaging.diagnosis: Acute posterior circulation ischemic stroke secondary to vertebral artery dissection
differential_by_imaging.key_features: DWI restriction in PICA territory with ipsilateral vertebral artery dissection on MRA and fat-sat T1
DWI restriction pattern and clinical acuity favor infarction over demyelination; no other white matter lesions
No bilateral cortical/subcortical edema in posterior regions; DWI restriction argues against PRES

recommended_actions: Urgent neurology consultation for posterior circulation stroke management
CTA head and neck to further characterize vertebral artery dissection extent
Initiate antithrombotic therapy per vertebral artery dissection protocol
Swallowing evaluation given lateral medullary involvement

findings: No evidence of pre-excitation or accessory pathway
ecg.interpretation: Normal sinus rhythm. No arrhythmia, conduction abnormality, or ischemic changes. No atrial fibrillation or other cardioembolic substrate identified on this tracing.
ecg.clinical_correlation: Normal ECG in a young stroke patient without traditional risk factors raises concern for cryptogenic stroke etiologies including vertebral artery dissection, paradoxical embolism through PFO, or hypercoagulable state. Prolonged cardiac monitoring recommended.

Lipid Panel.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated LDL, borderline cardiovascular risk factor

labs.interpretation: Laboratory results are essentially normal in this young patient. No evidence of diabetes, coagulopathy, inflammatory markers elevation, or metabolic derangement. The mildly elevated LDL is noted but unlikely the primary stroke etiology in this clinical context. Normal inflammatory markers argue against vasculitis or infectious etiology.

findings.location: Left vertebral artery V3 segment extending to V4 segment

signal_characteristics.CTA: Irregular narrowing of the left vertebral artery at V3-V4 with a tapered, flame-shaped occlusion pattern. Mural thickening with crescentic hyperdensity consistent with intramural hematoma. Residual lumen patent but significantly narrowed.

findings.location: Right vertebral artery, basilar artery, bilateral anterior circulation

signal_characteristics.CTA: Right vertebral artery is dominant and patent. Basilar artery patent without stenosis. Bilateral ICAs, MCAs, and ACAs are patent with normal caliber.

additional_observations: No pseudoaneurysm identified at the dissection site
Left PICA origin appears to arise from the dissected segment
output.impression: 1. Left vertebral artery dissection involving V3-V4 segments with significant luminal narrowing and intramural hematoma. The left PICA origin is at risk given its relationship to the dissected segment. 2. Remainder of the cervicocranial vasculature is normal. 3. Findings are consistent with traumatic or spontaneous vertebral artery dissection as the cause of the posterior circulation infarction.
recommended_actions: Initiate antithrombotic therapy (anticoagulation vs antiplatelet) per institutional vertebral artery dissection protocol
Follow-up vascular imaging in 3-6 months to assess for recanalization
Consider hypercoagulability workup given young age

Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.value: Trace tricuspid regurgitation, no other valvular abnormalities
Patent foramen ovale demonstrated with right-to-left shunting of agitated saline contrast within 3 cardiac cycles during Valsalva maneuver
Transthoracic Echocardiogram with Bubble.clinical_significance: Confirmed PFO with significant right-to-left shunt; however, vertebral artery dissection is the more likely stroke etiology in this clinical context
Interatrial septal hypermobility with PFO increases risk of paradoxical embolism, though role as primary etiology uncertain given confirmed dissection

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram with bubble study demonstrates normal biventricular function and no structural valvular disease. A patent foramen ovale is confirmed with significant right-to-left shunting during Valsalva maneuver, and mild interatrial septal hypermobility is noted. While the PFO could serve as a conduit for paradoxical embolism, the confirmed left vertebral artery dissection on CTA and MRA is the more likely embolic source in this clinical scenario. The PFO finding remains clinically relevant for long-term management discussions.
abnormal_values_summary: Patent foramen ovale with right-to-left shunt on bubble study

findings: Normal sinus rhythm throughout 48-hour recording period
No atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, or other supraventricular arrhythmias detected
Rare premature atrial complexes (18 in 48 hours, <0.1% burden)
output.interpretation: Forty-eight hour Holter monitoring reveals normal sinus rhythm throughout with no arrhythmias detected. No paroxysmal atrial fibrillation or other cardiac arrhythmia identified as a stroke etiology.
output.clinical_correlation: The absence of arrhythmia on prolonged monitoring, combined with the normal ECG, makes a cardioembolic mechanism from arrhythmia unlikely. The confirmed vertebral artery dissection remains the most likely stroke mechanism. The known PFO represents a potential secondary mechanism but is of uncertain clinical significance in this context.

Hypercoagulability Panel.clinical_significance: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden increases thrombotic risk 5-7 fold; may have contributed to thrombus formation at the dissection site or raises concern for paradoxical embolism through PFO

output.interpretation: Hypercoagulability panel reveals heterozygous Factor V Leiden mutation. This finding is clinically significant in the context of a young stroke patient with both a vertebral artery dissection and a patent foramen ovale. The Factor V Leiden mutation increases thrombotic risk and may contribute to thrombus formation at the dissection site. It also adds weight to considering PFO closure in the long-term management, as the combination of PFO with thrombophilia increases paradoxical embolism risk. Remaining hypercoagulability studies are within normal limits.
abnormal_values_summary: Factor V Leiden mutation: Heterozygous positive

findings.location: Bilateral common carotid arteries, internal and external carotid arteries

signal_characteristics.Right_vertebral: Patent with normal antegrade flow, dominant caliber
signal_characteristics.Left_vertebral: Dampened waveforms with reduced peak systolic velocity at the V2 segment, consistent with upstream flow-limiting pathology

additional_observations: No atherosclerotic plaque identified in either carotid bifurcation
Left vertebral artery flow abnormality correlates with known V3-V4 dissection
output.impression: 1. Normal bilateral carotid arteries without stenosis or plaque. 2. Abnormal left vertebral artery waveforms consistent with upstream dissection. 3. Dominant right vertebral artery with normal flow.
recommended_actions: Carotid findings do not suggest anterior circulation atherosclerotic disease
Left vertebral artery findings corroborate the CTA/MRA diagnosis of dissection

output.query: vertebral artery dissection stroke antithrombotic management anticoagulation vs antiplatelet

results.title: CADISS Trial: Antiplatelet treatment compared with anticoagulation treatment for cervical artery dissection
results.key_finding: No significant difference in efficacy between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy for cervical artery dissection at preventing stroke recurrence. Recurrence rate low in both arms.

results.title: Cervical Artery Dissection in Stroke Study (CADISS): Randomized trial long-term follow-up
results.key_finding: Extended follow-up confirms no difference between antiplatelet and anticoagulation therapy. Most recurrent events occur within the first 2 weeks.

results.title: AHA/ASA Guidelines: Management of Stroke Due to Cervical Artery Dissection
results.key_finding: Either antiplatelet or anticoagulation therapy is reasonable for extracranial cervical artery dissection. Treatment duration typically 3-6 months with follow-up vascular imaging.

output.summary: Current evidence from the CADISS trial and AHA/ASA guidelines does not demonstrate superiority of anticoagulation over antiplatelet therapy for cervical artery dissection. Both approaches show low recurrence rates. Treatment is typically continued for 3-6 months with repeat vascular imaging to assess healing. In the setting of coexisting PFO and thrombophilia, some experts may favor anticoagulation, but this requires individualized decision-making.

Connective Tissue Markers.clinical_significance: May be considered if clinical suspicion for Ehlers-Danlos syndrome persists
Borderline hypermobility, not meeting criteria for generalized joint hypermobility
Screening for Marfan syndrome if clinically indicated

output.interpretation: Bedside assessment for connective tissue disorder does not reveal definitive findings. Beighton score is 3/9, which is borderline but does not meet criteria for generalized joint hypermobility. No skin hyperextensibility or atrophic scarring noted. Genetic testing for fibrillin-1 mutations (Marfan syndrome) is pending. Given the vertebral artery dissection in a young patient following chiropractic manipulation, underlying connective tissue fragility cannot be excluded and genetic counseling may be warranted.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Acute ischemic stroke, left PICA territory, secondary to left vertebral artery dissection

differential.key_distinguishing: Direction-changing nystagmus and skew deviation on HINTS exam indicate a central rather than peripheral etiology; MRI confirms posterior circulation infarction
Patient has migraine history but symptoms are atypical for his usual migraines; persistent deficits beyond typical aura duration; MRI shows DWI restriction excluding migraine as sole cause
Hyperacute onset favors vascular over demyelinating etiology; DWI restriction pattern consistent with infarction; no other white matter lesions on MRI
Known PFO with demonstrated right-to-left shunt and heterozygous Factor V Leiden; however, vertebral artery dissection is the more proximate and likely embolic source
Vertigo is constant and not positional; negative Dix-Hallpike; diplopia and cerebellar signs not explained by BPPV

optimal_actions.action: Perform rapid neurological assessment including NIHSS and HINTS exam in the setting of acute vertigo with diplopia to distinguish central from peripheral vestibular syndrome
optimal_actions.expected_finding: NIHSS 3; HINTS exam shows direction-changing nystagmus and subtle skew deviation suggesting central etiology

optimal_actions.action: Activate stroke code based on central HINTS exam pattern and acute onset of vertigo with neurological signs, despite initial triage as vestibular neuritis
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Stroke team mobilized for emergent evaluation

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent non-contrast CT head to exclude hemorrhage
optimal_actions.expected_finding: CT likely negative as posterior fossa infarcts are often not visible on CT

optimal_actions.action: Obtain stat labs including CBC, BMP, coagulation studies, lipid panel, HbA1c, and troponin
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Labs essentially normal; no hyperglycemia, coagulopathy, or significant metabolic derangement

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate for arrhythmia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal sinus rhythm; no atrial fibrillation or other arrhythmia — cryptogenic stroke pattern in young patient

optimal_actions.action: Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI and MRA of head and neck, including fat-saturated T1 sequences for dissection evaluation
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Acute left lateral medullary infarction with left vertebral artery dissection on MRA and fat-sat T1

optimal_actions.action: Obtain CTA head and neck to characterize extent of vertebral artery dissection
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left vertebral artery V3-V4 dissection with significant luminal narrowing

optimal_actions.action: Initiate antithrombotic therapy — antiplatelet (aspirin or dual antiplatelet) or anticoagulation per institutional protocol for vertebral artery dissection
Perform swallowing evaluation given lateral medullary infarction risk of dysphagia
optimal_actions.expected_finding: May reveal mild dysphagia requiring dietary modifications

optimal_actions.action: Order transthoracic echocardiogram with bubble study to evaluate for PFO and cardiac source
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Confirmed PFO with right-to-left shunt; adds complexity to management but dissection is primary etiology

optimal_actions.action: Order hypercoagulability panel given young stroke and presence of PFO
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden — increases thrombotic risk and informs long-term management

optimal_actions.action: Admit to stroke unit for close neurological monitoring and prevention of stroke progression

critical_actions: Recognize that acute vertigo with direction-changing nystagmus and diplopia in a young patient represents a central vestibular syndrome requiring urgent stroke evaluation, not vestibular neuritis
Perform HINTS exam to differentiate central from peripheral vertigo — the direction-changing nystagmus and skew deviation are red flags for posterior circulation stroke
Obtain emergent brain MRI with DWI, as CT is frequently negative in posterior fossa strokes
Include MRA and fat-saturated T1 sequences to evaluate for vertebral artery dissection, especially given the history of recent cervical chiropractic manipulation
Initiate antithrombotic therapy for vertebral artery dissection to prevent stroke extension
Perform swallowing evaluation before oral intake given lateral medullary involvement
contraindicated_actions: Discharging the patient with a diagnosis of vestibular neuritis or BPPV without neuroimaging
Administering IV thrombolysis without first confirming the diagnosis and excluding dissection-related hemorrhagic complications (relative caution, not absolute contraindication)
Relying solely on CT to exclude posterior fossa stroke — CT has poor sensitivity for brainstem and cerebellar infarcts
Prescribing meclizine and discharging without further workup in a patient with central vestibular signs
Delaying MRI in favor of watchful waiting when central signs are present on HINTS exam
key_reasoning_points: Posterior circulation strokes frequently mimic benign peripheral vestibular disorders; the HINTS exam (Head Impulse, Nystagmus, Test of Skew) is a critical bedside tool to differentiate central from peripheral vertigo with 99% sensitivity for stroke when all three components suggest central etiology
Direction-changing nystagmus is a hallmark of central vestibular pathology and should never be attributed to vestibular neuritis, which causes unidirectional nystagmus

Young age with no traditional vascular risk factors should prompt investigation for vertebral artery dissection, especially when there is a history of cervical manipulation — this is a well-recognized precipitant

CT has notoriously poor sensitivity (approximately 40%) for posterior fossa infarcts; MRI with DWI is the imaging modality of choice and should be obtained urgently when posterior circulation stroke is suspected

The combination of PFO, Factor V Leiden, and vertebral artery dissection creates a complex clinical picture — the dissection is the proximate cause, but the thrombophilia and PFO inform long-term management decisions regarding anticoagulation duration and potential PFO closure

Lateral medullary syndrome (Wallenberg syndrome) presents with vertigo, nystagmus, ipsilateral cerebellar ataxia, and ipsilateral Horner syndrome, though not all features may be present in partial or early presentations
The CADISS trial showed no significant difference between anticoagulation and antiplatelet therapy for cervical artery dissection, allowing for individualized treatment decisions

metadata.difficulty_description: Diagnostic puzzle — young patient with acute vertigo, diplopia, and ataxia mimicking vestibular neuritis. Posterior circulation stroke secondary to vertebral artery dissection following chiropractic manipulation, complicated by PFO and Factor V Leiden. No traditional stroke risk factors.
clinical_pearls: The HINTS exam has higher sensitivity than MRI DWI in the first 24-48 hours for detecting posterior circulation stroke in acute vestibular syndrome
Vertebral artery dissection is the most common cause of posterior circulation stroke in patients under 45, with cervical manipulation being a recognized risk factor
Fatal posterior circulation strokes have been misdiagnosed as vestibular neuritis; always perform HINTS exam in acute vertigo presentations
The combination of PFO with atrial septal aneurysm and thrombophilia represents the highest-risk PFO phenotype for paradoxical embolism
